speaker: interviewer
text: Continuing our interview with Joseph Gringlas-- Joseph, while you were at the work camp, and then Birkenau, and then shipped to 'Biuno'--

speaker: interviewee
text: Buna.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Buna, did-- did you ever have dreams? Did you ever have visions of-- of something that-- did it feel like you were in a real place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I thought it was-- it was just like a dream. It is not true. It can't be true. You can't-- you can't get through those kind of time. You can't live through. You can't exist being like that, be treated like that. It's unbelievable. No. But they say if you have will of living, you-- I don't know how you define this. But we didn't think we gonna get out of it. It's no question about it. There's-- going back to Birkenau for a moment-- I remembered now. It just come to me. We were laying on the-- on the barracks. And some-- and at night, they came in with the SS-- a flashlight. And they took things-- people down and send to crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So next to me was laying a boy. I look around. He was gone. We saw them lighting the-- so unbelievable. He just-- he didn't pick me. He picked him. So I thought, maybe-- who-- who-- who's taking care of me to be-- to stay alive a little bit more, a little bit more? So it's terrible. It just-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What kinds of things did you think about? What kinds of things kept you going? What-- why--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. You go crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things kept you from just giving up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I think people, as long as they live, they want to still live. They want to-- they want to give up. But it was-- I don't know. There was-- talking about Birkenau, again, there were a lot of people-- you know, in Birkenau there was electric wires. Some people didn't-- went and got themselves to the wires and were killed right away. I don't know-- something-- a will of living. I don't know what that was. I-- I can't-- I can't explain it. It's hard to explain it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- did you think of your family? Did you have thoughts while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always thinking-- my family. I think that maybe that's what-- even when I am weak and all things, how we starved, but we're still alive. And we-- and we saved-- sudden moment we have of trauma or things happen, and-- and we could have been sent the same-- away to death. But we were saved. I don't know what happened, what-- what kept me from not going like the other people. So I thought maybe somebody is watching me.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You talked a little bit about nighttime. What-- what were nights like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't sleep. 'Cause-- cause we were hungry. You couldn't. And it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time-- were you working at night too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At night, we're not work. When night, we're laying in the barracks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did other people in the barracks share stories together, or prayers, or things that-- that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --kinda kept people going or encouraged? Did-- did you all have enough strength to kinda--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't

speaker: interviewer
text: --encourage each other?

speaker: interviewer
text: --or do you remember anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think there was encouragement, because everybody knew that this was the end of it. That was it. Nothing is gonna come out of that. And one thing we-- we-- we wished ourself-- that we were laying down on the barracks at night-- we shouldn't get up in the morning. And it would be the best thing. It would be all over. You don't have to suffer anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Back in Buna, did you-- you-- you were working, doing lighter work, after you'd had to do the heavy labor for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what type of lighter work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, it was helping with metal things, but not-- it was very light. It was not heavy work like I told you before what it was. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your kapos still criminal-- criminals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The other kapo was not. It was-- I think it was not a criminal, but it was different kapos there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they-- did you meet anyone that was a little more humane, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. There were some. And mostly, they're the one which were not murderers. They're trying to help out-- to-- to give you a little bit of food. Because the kapos said they were Germans. And they still always got some more food than what we got. So some, he was humane. He could-- we shared with him. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember one--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was one.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in particular who helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell his name-- it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was a German, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was he-- why was he there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. He just-- probably because there is something human in him. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Buna for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Buna, I was about-- I think it was the end of 1944. I think. There was-- I said in-- from-- from Birkenau to Buna we came-- it was a few months when we were-- it was almost the end of '44. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Buna for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --maybe a year, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was not a year. It was about, maybe, six months or something like that. Because I remember to Birkenau we came in June-- in May and June. We stayed there a few months. Then we're sent to Buna. And when we send away from Buna, at the end of the year. It was wintertime. And he-- and what happened in Buna, they-- after-- they want-- the Russians started getting-- moving from the east front. And the Germans-- running away. So they liquidated Buna. And-- and they took us-- not-- we walked eight kilometer at night, and some people-- in the snow. It was wintertime. And some people were killed by the guards, 'cause they couldn't walk. So they killed them right then on that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing in the snow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we're walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, walking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Walking. That was a next-- a place. It was called Gleiwitz-- G-L-E-I-W-I-T-Z. That wasn't eight kilometer-- eight kilometer from-- from Buna, when they liquidated Buna. So this place, Gleiwitz, they didn't bring just from Buna-- from all other places-- the people together. Because in the barracks was so full of people that you couldn't get in.

speaker: interviewer
text: All men at Gleiwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly men at that time, as I remember, yeah. And so we-- if-- if you got in, in the barracks, he would probably be-- and on top of the others-- would be killed. There were so many. So we went. We stayed in a toilet. Here we stayed. And then in the middle of the night, they were bringing trains. They were waiting for trains to come in. This was open trains-- 1/2 trains. And they were trying with the guns, all shooting us to get out fast, to load on on-- getting on the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was with my brother in-- getting to-- getting up on the train. And my-- one of the soldier hit with the gun from the other side from the wood piece-- right in the back of him. He was bleeding-- at the time, I remember. In the back of your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. If I-- hitting while they were running, so she goes so fast out. So they-- he was hit at that time. And we were loaded on that train. And that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was a train leaving Gleiwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leaving Gleiwitz, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this train going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know where we going, but we went to Germany. At that time we were in Gleiwitz. It's not far from Buna, which is Auschwitz, not-- around Krakow. Instead of going to Germany, they took us the whole-- it was 2 and a 1/2 weeks or 3 weeks, going to Poland, Czechoslovakia, around Czechoslovakia, to the German border, to Nordhausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was wintertime. And-- with-- they opened the trains. And they didn't feed us at all. What we ate is snow for two weeks. That was the only food we got. They didn't feed us. But what happened, when the train stopped on the certain stations-- and the SS was guarding on top of the trains. They were sitting there in special-- covered up, those, on the train for themselves. And they opened up on a station. And they knew the people didn't eat for a week or two. So they opened up the train, and they threw bread. The SS threw bread on the-- on the ground. And people were hungry, so they ran out. I was at the edge, not in the front. I couldn't run, because so many people-- one on top of the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where they're running out to catch the bread-- and they shoot them right from the top-- the SS-- those people. They killed right on the spot. This was the entertainment for them-- to see how they kill those people. What-- were there -- were there-- when you would stop at these train stations, were-- were there townspeople there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- well, we didn't-- one time it was during 5 o'clock in the morning, we went to a Czechoslovakian town. I don't know what the town name was it. But I remember there was a train going through. It was a bridge on top. It's a bridge-- a big bridge; and people walk-- civilian Czechoslovakian people going to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they knew-- they knew already what's going on and how they take those people to camps. And they throw out whatever they took with them-- lunch or break-- what they had there-- to walk to the factory. So they throw these apples, chocolate, candies-- throw it to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the SS was starting shooting to the bridges, because they saw they throwing the food to us. We caught something, I remember. My father caught an apple, caught a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this train was passing you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the train was going underneath, and the top was a bridge. And people walking to work like, 5, 6 o'clock in the morning, to the factories. They saw the bottom-- the train is going by. So whatever they had food with them, they throw it on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the top of your train was open.

speaker: interviewee
text: Open. It was open trains, yeah. If it wouldn't be open, I don't think we'd be able. Because so many people together, so there was air. It was cold, but snow, we had. That what we ate-- it was snow-- the whole winter--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people survived that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- this train coming to Nordhausen, which took 2 and a 1/2 weeks, I would say about 10%, 15% survived. I don't know how I survived just from snow eating. And I looked. When we came down Nordhausen, we looked-- I looked at my brother, and I saw to my brother, you know, we had told you you'd never survive anything. If we survived that, I think we gonna be alive. I couldn't believe that we survived that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how much did you weigh? Do you know? Do you-- can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --guess how much you must have weighed at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I weighed about 80-- 80, 90 pounds, or something like that. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] When you went to Nordhausen, when you got there, what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on the hills. I remember the barracks. But the barracks was not like in Auschwitz. It was like-- they used to-- they have-- those were barracks built from-- from bricks. And they was-- I hear that it was soldiers-- they were using it. It was kinda-- it was not that bad. It was kind-- it looked like a bed with wood made-- something where you laid in there. And-- and the first thing I remember when we went-- came to Nordhausen, they told us to go in to take showers. And it was wintertime, as I told you. We came in, and the uniform what we got, they didn't give us at that time when we took showers. We had to walk about a 1/2 a mile on the snow, naked, to go and get the uniform. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the way they treated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was this at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was at night-- that night, right. We came--

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you got your uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we give-- then went toward the barracks. And they gave us some soup and a piece of bread. It was all day long. And Nordhausen, what they took us-- we-- what we didn't know what's going on there That was the place-- Nordhausen-- in the mountains that they built V1, V2. During the war they had the bombs, the missiles in London. And that's what was the first place where they made those missiles. And we didn't know. We just-- we were-- helped out unloading this. But we didn't know what was going on there. One time, I remember that there was a civilian engineer there, at Nordhausen. He was a German. He looked around. He look around and back. He had a piece of bread. He gave me that bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Was he a prisoner, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he wasn't-- he wasn't a prisoner. He was a civilian. He was an engineer working there. But he looked around to make sure there's nobody sees him-- what he's doing. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS] What kind of work did you do at Nordhausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I told you, helping out the cementing the floor. They put machines for all kinds of missile they built. They even used soldiers I knew of to helping work there. They were so rushing when they do have this done real fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some soldiers in uniform-- they were not SS-- soldiers coming furlough, from the front or whatever, they were send for working there, too with us. Hm. Did you-- did they talk to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn anything about the war? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --find out anything about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: But what we learned about the war, that they were starting to say that's-- we knew already when they took us out in Poland-- the Russians starting-- getting for-- taking over Poland, back. But we were in Germany at that time. But it was coming-- alarms at night. They made alarms in the like, bombs to try-- we knew it's come from England or American-- bombs, bombardment for the Nordhausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea that Germans might be close to losing the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had a feeling that it's getting close, but they're not gonna let us out to survive. But then at-- later on, they didn't take me to work any more. They set up a special medical instruments. I don't know. We had to work through. And I was-- I wasn't able to do any work, so they didn't take me to work. They left me in the barrack. And I don't think that they want to use an ammunition to kill, because we gonna die, anyways. They might need ammunition to-- on the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't kill those people, because they know we gonna die, anyway. And that lasted there-- a month or so. And then it was starting bombardment, coming down often. When-- when you were left in the barracks--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you left to die there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I think so. Because they-- if they didn't take you to work, then-- that they was not-- it was-- I was not assigned to work anymore. My brother was, still, but not me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you survive? Were-- how did you eat? Were you given food?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave you, still, that portion of soup and bread in the evening. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a kapo at Nordhausen? Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was kapos at Nordhausen too. Yeah, there were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it-- did they have crematoriums there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did-- in-- in Birkenau-- I mean, excuse me-- in Nordhausen, I didn't see no crematoriums. But then I found that there were some crematoriums away from there. But I didn't see any crematoriums, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to go through selections at Nordhausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- at that-- no. There was no-- appells, like, usually they had to do. But there was no selection. But, of course, people died. They-- they starved to death. And they buried them or something. I don't know what happened. There, maybe they took them. There were some-- I heard about there was a crematorium not far away, but I didn't see it. I didn't see any crematoriums there. And there was some working on the mountains-- for something about planes, or planes. And they were terrible, in terrible condition. But I didn't see no crematoriums there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long were you in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there-- as I told you, there we starting getting more often bombardment. And on one night we went-- it was a bombardment. So the SS run out too, out of the camps, out in the roads, because they were afraid they were gonna-- and then what happened, the bombardment quiet down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where would we go? We had no place to go. We went back to the-- to the camp, because we had uniforms. Where you gonna go? You can't go no place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that night, they took a lot of people out to be killed, because they know that's the end of it. They didn't want to-- and I-- and we were not-- we were not taken out. We saw them marching away, a lot of other-- the separate grouping taken out to be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people did the Germans take out?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took out a few hundred at that time, you know, just to be killed. That's what we saw. We know the way they killing around. But then, the next morning, it start to come bombardment. It was 10 o'clock in the morning. And I was-- and I-- and went out, again-- out the camps. And I was outside, I would say a-- maybe, a 1/4 of a mile away from the camp, laying on the ground. And next to me was an SS laying right next to me. And he said in German, [GERMAN]. That means, it's-- we finished-- that's what-- in German. But I felt good. I didn't care for him to live or not. But I saw him next to me. He was even with me-- SS-- laying with me. And I was-- at that time, the bombardment was all-- the whole-- everything. It was 10 o'clock in the morning. It looked like night.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you have enough strength to leave the barracks to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just-- when you feel like it's danger, you run. You can. It's unbelievable. You just walked out. I don't know how we did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were laying down--

speaker: interviewee
text: We're laying down.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to-- to not-- so you would not get hit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Next-- me with SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to an SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And what happened-- and when I got up, and it was-- I was injured from the shrapnel from the bombardment. And it's still in my lungs. And my brother was injured in the leg. And that few days, there was no Germans. There was nobody. And we were just yearning from that pains, what we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went into a place for all these injured people, where they're laying. And then in-- during the night, came in civilian Germans with guns, and take him out. But still, they know they gonna be still alive-- killed them out. And then we-- we-- Civilian-- I'm-- civilian Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: Civilian Germans--

speaker: interviewer
text: --came

speaker: interviewee
text: --yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --into the-- where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, into the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --everyone was sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: --into those-- the laying people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They killed the sick people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saw that they still more alive than the other ones, yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, these were not people--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, these weren't SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you say German civilians--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what they were. I didn't see the uniform now.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were people from the town or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, probably from town or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So next morning, what happened, I just went down, and I saw first the American tanks coming into that Nordhausen. So my brother was injured in the leg. And I felt, after a night, I was-- couldn't breathe from the injury. But next morning, I felt a little bit better-- quiet down, didn't bleed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans-- and the Americans came in. And they were running. And they screamed, the Americans came! We are liberated! And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what date was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was April the 11, 1945. And they came in. They were like brothers to us. They helped set-- they set up a medical on the field. They carry out my-- my-- my brother to a-- to a hospital. They tried to save his leg. It was green already from the injury. And I was-- they put an x-ray on me. They say I was very happy that the injury would have been through right by my heart. But it split from my heart. So I don't know what happened. It was meant I should stay alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the-- what was your first contact with the Americans when they came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one contact was-- it was American soldiers. They tried-- like your own brother would find you. They tried to help with everything-- with medicine, with food. And a lot of people, they didn't know themselves how-- they was eating a lot of meat. And they were-- they died. 'Cause they couldn't survive, because their stomach didn't take it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what were you given to eat? Did you eat right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or were you given liquids?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- yeah. When I felt a little better, I went-- organize some jams, I remember, jams-- to very light food, because if I'm gonna eat-- I saw there were dying people. So that's what I-- little by little. And at that time, I was about 80 pounds-- in that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed in that hospital till we got well, on our feet, and-- And what did the Americans say to you? Did they know that had been a labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, they-- they told us they didn't know it was a labor camp, because, I told you, the barracks were built of bricks. So they bombarded. They thought it's a-- the-- the German soldiers, German SS. And a lot of the people from that Nordhausen were killed from bombardment. That evening-- I told you, I went back. We saw a lot of people are hit right in the corner of the building. And there were people where-- which you saw before, were burning like coals because from the bombardment. They-- the Americans didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what-- how did the Americans react to the situation of what they saw there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one-- I remember one American soldier was-- he said, he-- and he like from German, like grandparents or what. He said, I am ashamed to say I came out from Germans. He saw what-- what they did to our people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many people survived Nordhausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. There were survived, maybe, a few hundred. I don't know what-- how many. Because we went away. We start to-- and after that, we saw the Americans coming. And we went and we tored off my uniforms. And we found there something, a place. There was S-- pilots-- you-- pilots' jackets. We stripped all the insignia from the pilots-- what it says. And we-- I got a picture of that. I showed you that picture. We took a picture with my brother. We had those pilots' jackets, because I tore right away, off. We didn't want to have that-- those-- the stripes, so we-- people should see right away where we were. But they could tell. Looking at us, you could tell where we were. And the German civilians starting telling. They didn't know anything about that-- what's been done. They didn't know anything. They took-- saw people were marching to work, to working. And they-- they knew exactly what that-- but they try-- they trying to tell us that they were not cooperating with them. They didn't help them. They didn't know what went on. And what happened, a lot of people died. So the madness made them-- the Germans coming, the civilians who looking all those camps, what the Germans did, and buried those-- those people that were laying not buried-- the dead ones which didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and Saul were then at-- at the camp for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in camp a few months there till we got by ourself strength back.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a temporary--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --clinic or hospital there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I told you, we went-- went back to Poland to see if anybody is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you left Nordhausen,

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you when you went back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your home town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we ran away from Poland, because we could-- because I had saw in Poland they are anti-Semites and started killing people that came home to look for--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you-- how did you get from Nordhausen to Ostrowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, by train-- by-- by a train, because it's less. We sat on the train with loading the coals on the locomotives. We sat right on top of that. So we came like, all black.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you jumped on a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: For two nights. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did know that that was going to Ostrowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we knew that's going to Poland. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because they signed with-- when they see maybe, look. Somebody is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take you to get to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took a few nights-- a few days and nights, because, you know, they're going slow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you arrived in your town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: The town was the same thing as we left, you know. But then we talked to the-- one of the gentile people. And he said, you survived? And we saw what-- we heard what's going on. There's-- there's a lot of-- there were something called AK, Armia Kryova. Those were really those against the-- trying to kill the Jewish people. So-- and we--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the war had ended--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --but there were still groups killing the Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when they went back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And that what's happened. We had pogroms in Poland. In Kielce there were killed 40 people from my-- from the camp when they came back to look for anybody who's alive. They were killed in Kielce.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you found out about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we ran to Warsaw. And we joined a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kibbutz was in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Warsaw. And how did you get from your hometown to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- we went from my hometown by a train. The trains were running already at that time. But I was told that there was a lot of happened on trains. Many of those-- it was with the guns-- civilians-- they were civilians, but they call themselves-- they were looking for the people that like us. And they killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- I-- I didn't know that by that time. But I arrived. And he said, you were lucky that they didn't get you. Because if you look Jewish, they would take you down and kill you. That was after the war, coming home to my hometown where I was born, and was raised. So we-- we went there. And we run, both us-- we went to Warsaw, because we thought, a little town, a bigger city, maybe we could survive more people together. So we joined a kibbutz there. And what was the kibbutz like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kibbutz was-- the kibbutz at the time was-- was just-- it was the only way to go is to Israel, to Palestine. At that time there was no Israel. It was Palestine. So it's the only place to go. We couldn't stay in Poland. It was no use to stay in Poland. We had been through hell. And we, I mean, they don't even want you to be there. They wanted to kill the people in the-- in the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we wanna go to some country where we be able to live. So the only thing is going to Palestine. In a way, kibbutz were organized to go-- for that group to go over there.
